solution.p: We will factor 4r^{2}-4r+1 where a=4, b=-4, and c=1.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial 4r^{2}-4r+1 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]4r^{2}-4r+1\amp =\left(2r\right)^2-2\left(2r\right)\left(1\right)+\left(1\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(2r-1\right)\mathopen{}\left(2r-1\right)\newline \amp =\left(2r-1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (4) \cdot (1) = 4.

The number 4 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(4)\amp -1(-4)\amp 2(2)\amp -2(-2)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -4, and this sum happens with (-2)+(-2)=-4 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]4r^{2}-4r+1\amp =4r^{2}-2r-2r+1
ewline \amp =2r\mathopen{}\left(2r-1ight)-\left(2r-1ight)
ewline \amp =\left(2r-1ight)\mathopen{}\left(2r-1ight)
ewline \amp =\left(2r-1ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 4r^{2}-4r+1 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 4, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(4)\amp 2(2)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp -1(-1)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 4r^{2}-4r+1 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]4r^{2}-4r+1\amp =\left(2r-1\right)\mathopen{}\left(2r-1\right)\newline \amp =\left(2r-1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
